query: low fsh level and pituitary function tests

retrieved_docs.title: Kallmann Syndrome -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Testing for Suspected Kallmann Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: In cases of hypogonadism, testosterone levels in males are typically <100 ng/dL, and estradiol levels in females are <50 pg/mL, along with low FSH and LH levels. [6] Other findings may include the following: Low levels of inhibin B (<35 pg/mL) suggest GnRH deficiency rather than CDGP and rule out primary testicular failure. [51] [52] [53] [53] Insulin-like growth factor 1 can be used to assess growth hormone levels. If it is normal, a growth hormone deficiency is unlikely. The hCG stimulation test may help differentiate Kallmann syndrome from constitutional delay of growth and puberty. Cortisol, prolactin, thyroid hormones, ACTH, insulin-like growth factor, and growth hormones are generally normal in GnRH deficiency and Kallmann syndrome. Additional tests may include a semen analysis, bone density testing, renal ultrasonography, and a brain MRI. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Pituitary Cancer -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Hyposecretory adenomas are tumors that cause the hyposecretion of hormones from the pituitary gland and can involve one or more hormones. Hyposecreting tumors require measuring levels of multiple hormones such as thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), luteinizing hormone (LH), free T4, total testosterone (in men), and cortisol. Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism requires magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain at the time of diagnosis to rule out a pituitary tumor as the underlying etiology. Confirmatory testing with stimulatory testing follows basic laboratory testing, and these tests are specific to the hormone of interest. [27] [28] [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Testosterone -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: If a clinician expects hypogonadism based on history and physical, a total serum testosterone between 8 AM and 10 AM should be drawn. Normal levels may indicate eugonadal low testosterone. If levels are low, a repeat level and FSH and LH levels should be obtained. Low testosterone in the setting of normal FSH/LH indicates secondary hypogonadism. The next steps would be to get prolactin, T4, 8 AM cortisol, iron, ferritin levels, and a brain MRI. Low testosterone in the setting of elevated FSH/LH indicated primary hypogonadism. In the case of primary hypogonadism, a karyotype should be established.

retrieved_docs.title: Kallmann Syndrome -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Testing for Suspected Kallmann Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory testing for suspected Kallmann syndrome should be conducted to exclude unrelated hormonal deficiencies, pituitary tumors, and other systemic syndromes and to diagnose Kallmann syndrome. The testing includes LH, FSH, testosterone or estradiol, prolactin, free T4, thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH), and cortisol. [5] More specialized tests may also be performed, including serum adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH), ferritin, inhibin B, anti-Mullerian hormone, insulin-like 3, GnRH, inhibin B, insulin-like growth factor 1, and the human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) stimulation test. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Male Infertility -- Evaluation -- Other Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Low testosterone with normal or low FSH and LH indicates secondary hypogonadism. Check serum prolactin.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypopituitarism -- Treatment / Management -- FSH/LH deficit
retrieved_docs.content: In women, estrogen and progesterone hormone replacement therapy can be administered IM, orally, or transdermally. Hormone levels should be carefully monitored and normalized across all axes in this group, not just for the reproductive system, especially when fertility is desired. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Sheehan Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluation of anterior pituitary function is done by obtaining blood when a high clinical suspicion is evident to diagnose Sheehan syndrome. The hormones that are manufactured in the anterior pituitary gland are the gonadotropins such as follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and luteinizing hormone (LH), growth hormone (GH), prolactin (PRL), adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH), and thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH). They are affected primarily in a certain order when necrosis occurs, GH first, followed by PRL, FSH, LH, ACTH, and then TSH last. [1] Laboratory tests to order would include a complete blood count (CBC) with differential count, basic metabolic profile, thyroid function tests (TSH, FT3, FT4), FSH, LH, prolactin, estrogen, cortisol, and growth hormone. [8] Finding a low basal hormone level along with a history and physical suggestive of Sheehan syndrome would help to make the diagnosis. Other laboratory tests that may occur with Sheehan syndrome include normocytic/normochromic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and/or pancytopenia. Hyponatremia and hypoglycemia may also be present as well. Other less common laboratory tests in the workup for Sheehan syndrome that may be abnormal include multiple thrombophilic genetic mutations, low PT and aPTT tests, anti-pituitary antibodies, and anti-hypothalamus antibodies. [9] These tests may be considered after the primary tests to confirm the diagnosis. Also, a magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) evaluation of the pituitary can be done to confirm the diagnosis. An empty sella is present in about 70% of patients, and a partially empty sella is present in about 30% of patients on a later MRI. [10] Acute Sheehan syndrome signs on an MRI may demonstrate acute central infarction without hemorrhage in an enlarged pituitary. As the disease progresses, the MRI may show pituitary gland atrophy and eventually a partial or empty sella. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Secondary Amenorrhea -- Evaluation -- Hypogonadotropic findings
retrieved_docs.content: Although FHA is considered a diagnosis of exclusion, low FSH and E2, in the setting of normal prolactin and thyroid function tests in patients with a potential risk factor for FHA (eg, low body mass index, competitive athlete) strongly suggests FHA as the most likely etiology. Additional serum testing, including a comprehensive metabolic panel, complete blood count, and C-reactive protein, can be ordered in these patients. A baseline dual-energy x-ray absorptiometry is also often appropriate, especially if amenorrhea has been prolonged. [7] Patients should be offered appropriate counseling and interventions, as HPO axis function can be restored once the underlying stressor is corrected. Patients with hypogonadotropic amenorrhea who do not respond to initial management strategies may require brain imaging to evaluate the hypothalamus and pituitary gland further.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Follicle Stimulating Hormone -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated levels of FSH are associated with unresponsive gonads or hyperfunctioning pituitary adenomas. Low levels of FSH are associated with either hypothalamic or anterior pituitary dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: Pituitary Adenoma -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Gonadotropin levels (LH/FSH): Low estradiol or testosterone levels with normal or low LH/FSH levels suggest hypogonadotropic hypogonadism. The sex hormones can not be interpreted accurately in females if they are on oral contraceptives. Post-menopausal females will physiologically have elevated FSH levels.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperprolactinemia -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Following tests are generally needed depending upon gender, age, and clinical presentation: Serum prolactin Thyroid function test Renal function test Insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) Adrenocorticotrophic hormone (ACTH) Luteinizing hormone (LH) Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) Testosterone/estradiol Pregnancy test

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Amenorrhea -- Evaluation -- Interpretation of Initial Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Serum testing: It is always important to first rule out pregnancy, as a patient ovulates before their first period and, therefore, may become pregnant before menarche. [9] The FSH and estradiol levels can help distinguish primary hypothalamic and pituitary abnormalities from primary ovarian etiologies. If the screening FSH level is low, the diagnosis of hypogonadotropic hypogonadism can be confirmed, indicating the primary defect is in the hypothalamus or pituitary gland. If the FSH level is elevated, this indicates hypergonadotropic hypogonadism, pointing to a primary defect in the ovary which is unable to respond to normal gonadotropin secretion. [29] [6] Since estrogen suppresses FSH release, estradiol is also usually ordered to interpret the FSH level correctly. Estrogen levels are typically low in primary hypothalamic, pituitary, and ovarian disorders, while normal FSH and estradiol levels are seen with structural anomalies.

retrieved_docs.title: Secondary Amenorrhea -- Evaluation -- Interpretation of Initial Evaluation Findings and Further Testing
retrieved_docs.content: The FSH and estradiol levels can help identify functional abnormalities in the hypothalamus, pituitary, and ovary (see Table . Type of Hypogonadism and Levels of FSH and E2). Depending on the etiology, secondary amenorrhea can be classified as hypogonadotropic, hypergonadotropic, or eugonadotropic, based on the gonadotropin (ie, FSH) level, which helps the clinician pinpoint the location of the abnormality. For example, in secondary amenorrhea caused by a primary defect in the hypothalamus or pituitary, FSH secretion is low, resulting in low E2 production in the ovary. Conversely, in POI, the ovary cannot produce E2 regardless of the stimulation it gets; low estrogen levels signify a lack of feedback inhibition in the pituitary, and FSH levels are elevated. The pituitary is "yelling'" at the ovaries to make estrogen, but the ovaries cannot respond to the call.) FHA, functional hypothalamic amenorrhea; PCOS, polycystic ovarian syndrome; POI, premature ovarian insufficiency

retrieved_docs.title: Luteinizing Hormone Deficiency -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory tests: The initial evaluation aims to rule out hypothyroidism by measuring serum TSH levels and pregnancy in women. Serum levels of LH, along with FSH and prolactin (PRL), are measured. Pituitary tumors that extend superior to pituitary stalk and suprasellar tumors impair dopamine delivery to the pituitary stalk. They prevent dopamine inhibition of prolactin secretion from lactotrophs and cause hyperprolactinemia. [14] Olfactory testing is used in Kallmann syndrome to diagnose anosmia. MRI of the head can help to study structural abnormalities in the hypothalamus and pituitary, especially pituitary adenomas.

retrieved_docs.title: Craniopharyngioma -- Evaluation -- Endocrine Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Given the tumor’s proximity to the hypothalamic-pituitary axis, comprehensive hormonal testing is necessary. Laboratory tests should include: Morning fasting cortisol and adrenocorticotropic hormone Evaluates adrenal insufficiency Thyroid-stimulating hormone and free T4 Assesses for central hypothyroidism Gonadotropins (follicle-stimulating hormone [FSH], luteinizing hormone [LH]), estradiol (females), testosterone (males) Identifies hypogonadotropic hypogonadism Growth hormone and insulin-like growth factor-1 Screens for growth hormone deficiency, commonly seen in children Prolactin May be mildly elevated due to the pituitary stalk effect Serum sodium, urine specific gravity, and osmolality Evaluates for diabetes insipidus (vasopressin deficiency) Cosyntropin stimulation test Performed when morning cortisol is equivocal or indeterminate to assess adrenal reserve

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Follicle Stimulating Hormone -- Clinical Significance -- Hypogonadotropic Hypogonadism
retrieved_docs.content: In cases of amenorrhea with low levels of FSH and LH, the fault lies with either the hypothalamus or the pituitary gland. Stress-Induced Hypogonadotropic Hypogonadism

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 673
retrieved_docs.content: Somatorelin and Metyrapon are both classified as pituitary function test diagnostic agents. As members of this group, they are used to evaluate the activity of the pituitary gland, helping clinicians assess hormone secretion and diagnose endocrine disorders that involve pituitary dysfunction. Their inclusion in the category of pituitary function test diagnostic agents highlights their specific role in testing and monitoring pituitary performance in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2719
retrieved_docs.content: Follitropin beta is classified within the structural class gonadotropiny, indicating that it is a member of the gonadotropin family of hormones that regulate reproductive function. This classification reflects its molecular similarity to other gonadotropins such as follicle‑stimulating hormone (FSH) and luteinizing hormone (LH), and underlies its clinical use in assisted reproductive technologies to stimulate ovarian follicle development. Understanding that follitropin beta belongs to the gonadotropiny structural class helps clinicians predict its pharmacologic behavior, therapeutic indications, and potential interactions with other agents that affect the hypothalamic‑pituitary‑gonadal axis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2279
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone Agonist is classified as a hormone and related agent.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1893
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytocin and its analogues are classified as posterior pituitary lobe hormones.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1614
retrieved_docs.content: Sermorelin and metyrapon are both classified as pituitary function test diagnostic agents, indicating that each is used to assess the activity of the pituitary gland. As members of the broader category of pituitary function test diagnostic agents, sermorelin and metyrapon serve in clinical protocols to evaluate hormone secretion and diagnose pituitary disorders, underscoring their role in endocrine diagnostic testing.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1421
retrieved_docs.content: Pituitary function test diagnostic agents constitute a class of compounds employed to evaluate the activity of the pituitary gland and to aid in the diagnosis of pituitary disorders. This class includes the agents somatorelin, sermorelin, and metyrapon, each of which can be used in diagnostic testing to assess hormone secretion and pituitary function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1418
retrieved_docs.content: PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES are classified as hypothalamic hormones, encompassing both the natural hormones secreted by the pituitary and hypothalamus and their synthetic analogues. This categorization places them within the broader group of hypothalamic hormones, highlighting their shared origin and functional relevance in endocrine regulation and therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1303
retrieved_docs.content: PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES is defined as an overarching class of endocrine agents that includes both anterior pituitary lobe hormones and analogues as well as hypothalamic hormones (hypotalamické hormony). In this hierarchy, anterior pituitary lobe hormones and analogues are considered a subset of the broader PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES group, reflecting the close functional relationship between pituitary secretions and their regulatory hypothalamic counterparts. At the same time, the entire PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES category is regarded as a type of hypothalamic hormone group, underscoring the integrated nature of pituitary‑hypothalamic endocrine pathways and the inclusion of synthetic analogues that mimic these natural hormones. This comprehensive classification aids in understanding the shared physiology, therapeutic targeting, and clinical management of disorders involving pituitary and hypothalamic hormone systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2955
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone (LHRH) agonists constitute a class of compounds that stimulate the gonadotropin‑releasing hormone receptor to modulate pituitary hormone release, and triptorelin is a specific agent that belongs to this LHRH‑agonist class. Recognizing triptorelin as a member of the LHRH‑agonist group is essential for understanding its mechanism of action and therapeutic applications in hormone‑dependent conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1417
retrieved_docs.content: ACTH and synthetic analog preparations are classified as anterior pituitary lobe hormones and analogues, indicating that they belong to the group of hormones produced by the anterior pituitary gland as well as their synthetic counterparts. Cosyntropin is identified as a member of the ACTH and synthetic analog preparations group, representing a specific synthetic ACTH analogue used in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1464
retrieved_docs.content: Choriogonadotropin and follitropin beta are both classified within the gonadotropiny structural class, indicating that they share a common protein architecture characteristic of gonadotropin hormones. As members of this structural class, both agents exhibit similar molecular features that enable them to interact with gonadal receptors and influence reproductive processes. Recognizing that choriogonadotropin and follitropin beta belong to the same gonadotropin family helps clinicians understand their comparable mechanisms of action, potential cross‑reactivity, and appropriate therapeutic uses in assisted reproduction and related endocrine treatments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 374
retrieved_docs.content: amino acids (γ‑aminobutyric acid, glutamic acid, leucine, arginine), botanical extracts, and animal tissues (adrenal gland, pituitary, thyroid). These products are marketed for diverse therapeutic aims, including allergy mitigation, neuro‑behavioral support, appetite and weight regulation, thyroid‑adrenal axis support, and general metabolic or endocrine balancing, reflecting a strategy of synergistic modulation of endocrine, autonomic and metabolic pathways. In addition, epinephrine is formulated as a 1 mg/mL injectable solution, reinforcing its role as a rapid‑acting catecholamine. Glucagon is another peptide hormone belonging to the amino‑acid/peptide hormone class. Gonadotropins—specifically choriogonadotropin (human chorionic gonadotropin, hCG) and follitropin‑beta—regulate reproductive function; hCG is also employed clinically as a tumor biomarker because elevated levels can indicate certain malignancies. Normetanefrin, the catecholamine metabolite, similarly serves as a tumor

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2767
retrieved_docs.content: Lidský choriogonadotropin, commonly referred to as human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG), is recognized as a tumor biomarker (nádorové biomarkery). In clinical practice, hCG levels are measured to aid in the detection, diagnosis, and monitoring of certain malignancies, as elevated concentrations can indicate the presence of tumor activity. Consequently, lidský choriogonadotropin serves as an important biochemical marker that contributes to oncological assessment and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1601
retrieved_docs.content: Estradiol is classified as a hormone, reflecting its role as a key endogenous steroid that regulates reproductive physiology. Gonadotropiny, representing the family of gonadotropins, is a subclass within the broader hormone category, indicating that gonadotropins share fundamental hormonal properties. The group termed “Natural and semisynthetic estrogens, plain” includes estradiol as one of its members, situating estradiol among both naturally occurring and synthetically derived estrogenic compounds used in clinical practice. Choriogonadotropin and follitropin beta are both identified as having the structural class gonadotropiny, meaning they belong to the gonadotropin family and share similar molecular architecture and biological functions related to stimulation of the gonads. Together, these relationships delineate how estradiol fits within the hormone hierarchy, how gonadotropins are organized as a hormonal subclass, and how specific agents such as choriogonadotropin and follitropin beta are structurally related to the gonadotropin class, informing both pharmacologic classification and therapeutic application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2736
retrieved_docs.content: Choriogonadotropin is classified within the structural class of gonadotropiny, indicating that it belongs to the gonadotropin family of hormones.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 58
retrieved_docs.content: Castration is a therapeutic intervention that targets the endocrine system. Pituitary function test diagnostic agents also include somatorelin, sermorelin, seromorelin and metyrapon; sermorelin and seromorelin belong to the somatropin and somatropin agonist class. The class “PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES” is categorized as a type of hypothalamic hormones. Together, these hierarchical and functional relationships illustrate the integration of hormone analogues, diagnostic agents, therapeutic interventions and imaging studies within classification systems to support clinical evaluation and targeted patient management.
